title: Alkenes — double bonds, addition reactions and polymers
examTip: Alkenes are unsaturated (contain C=C double bond). General formula CnH2n. Test with bromine water — alkenes decolourise it (orange to colourless). Addition reactions: the double bond opens up and two atoms add across it. Addition polymerisation makes polymers from alkene monomers.
introTips: The double bond is what makes alkenes reactive — it is the site where addition reactions happen.
Bromine water is the test for unsaturation: if it decolourises (orange → colourless) the compound is an alkene. Alkanes do not react with bromine water.
Addition reactions: one molecule is added across the double bond, which opens up to form a new single bond.
CnH2n is the general formula — compare with alkanes (CnH2n+2): alkenes have two fewer hydrogens because of the double bond.
Ethene (C₂H₄) is the most important alkene to know — it is the monomer for poly(ethene) and is produced by cracking.
intro: Alkenes are where organic chemistry starts to get interesting. Unlike alkanes, which are fairly unreactive because all their bonds are single bonds, alkenes have a carbon-carbon double bond that is ready to react. That double bond is the reason alkenes can do something alkanes cannot: addition reactions, where other molecules join directly onto the carbon chain. This reactivity makes alkenes incredibly useful as starting materials — from making plastics to producing alcohols. Ethene alone is one of the most important industrial chemicals in the world.

keyPoints.heading: Structure and general formula of alkenes
keyPoints.content:
Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons — they contain a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C), which means they have fewer hydrogens than the equivalent alkane.

General formula: CnH2n

First three alkenes:
• Ethene: C₂H₄ (n=2) — important industrial chemical, monomer for poly(ethene)
• Propene: C₃H₆ (n=3) — monomer for poly(propene)
• Butene: C₄H₈ (n=4)

The C=C double bond is always between the first and second carbon in the simplest alkenes. The double bond consists of one sigma bond (like a single bond) and one pi bond — the pi bond is the reactive part that opens up in addition reactions.

You can draw the displayed formula: for ethene, each carbon has 2 H atoms and a double bond to the other carbon.
writeBullets: Alkenes: unsaturated hydrocarbons with at least one C=C double bond
General formula: CnH2n
Ethene C₂H₄, propene C₃H₆, butene C₄H₈
Double bond makes alkenes much more reactive than alkanes
Displayed formula of ethene: H₂C=CH₂

keyTerms.def: Contains at least one C=C double bond; can undergo addition reactions
An unsaturated hydrocarbon with at least one C=C double bond; general formula CnH2n
A double bond between two carbon atoms, consisting of a sigma bond and a pi bond; the reactive site in alkenes

keyPoints.memTip: Alkane = satisfied (saturated, no double bonds, all single). Alkene = excited (unsaturated, has a double bond, ready to react).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students must be able to draw displayed formulas for ethene and propene and recognise the C=C bond. Emphasise that unsaturated means something is 'missing' and can react to add more atoms.

keyPoints.heading: Testing for alkenes — bromine water
keyPoints.content:
The bromine water test is used to distinguish saturated compounds (alkanes) from unsaturated compounds (alkenes).

Method: Add a few drops of bromine water (orange/brown) to the compound and shake.

Results:
• Alkene present: Bromine water is decolourised (orange/brown → colourless) — bromine has added across the double bond: C=C + Br₂ → −CBr−CBr−
• Alkane (or no double bond) present: Bromine water remains orange/brown — no reaction at room temperature.

This is an addition reaction — bromine adds across the double bond, which is converted to a single bond. The product is a dibromoalkane.
writeBullets: Bromine water test: orange bromine water + alkene → decolourises (colourless)
Alkanes: bromine water stays orange — no reaction
Decolourisation shows unsaturation (C=C double bond present)
Reaction: C=C + Br₂ → −CBr−CBr− (dibromoalkane formed)
This is an addition reaction — Br₂ adds across the double bond

keyTerms.def: Orange bromine water is decolourised by alkenes (addition reaction) but not by alkanes; used to test for unsaturation
The product of adding bromine across a C=C double bond; contains two C−Br bonds

keyPoints.memTip: Orange in, colourless out = alkene (it grabbed the bromine). Orange stays orange = alkane (not reactive enough). Think: alkene 'drinks' the bromine colour.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Insist students describe the colour change fully in exams: 'orange/brown to colourless' — just 'it goes colourless' loses marks. They must also state what the colour change shows (unsaturation / C=C present).

keyPoints.heading: Addition reactions of alkenes
keyPoints.content:
Alkenes undergo addition reactions — the C=C double bond opens up and two atoms (or groups of atoms) add across it to form a single product with no atoms left over.

Key addition reactions:

1. + Br₂ (bromine water/gas): C=C + Br₂ → −CBr−CBr− (dibromoalkane) — used as a test for unsaturation

2. + H₂ (hydrogenation): C=C + H₂ → C−C (alkane) — requires nickel catalyst, 150°C. Used to make margarine from vegetable oils.

3. + HBr (hydrogen bromide): C=C + HBr → −CHBr− (bromoalkane) — electrophilic addition

4. + H₂O (steam, hydration): C=C + H₂O → −COH− (alcohol) — requires phosphoric acid catalyst, 300°C, 60 atm. Used to make ethanol industrially.

All produce a single product — no by-products.
writeBullets: Addition reaction: C=C opens up, one molecule adds across to give a single product
+ Br₂: decolourises bromine water → dibromoalkane
+ H₂ (hydrogenation): alkane formed; Ni catalyst, 150°C
+ HBr: bromoalkane formed
+ H₂O (steam, hydration): alcohol formed; H₃PO₄ catalyst, 300°C, 60 atm
Addition reactions always give one product — no by-products

keyTerms.def: A reaction where two molecules combine to form one product; the C=C double bond opens up and atoms are added across it
Addition of H₂ across a C=C double bond; converts an alkene to an alkane; requires a nickel catalyst
Addition of water (H₂O) across a C=C double bond; converts an alkene to an alcohol; requires steam and a phosphoric acid catalyst

keyPoints.memTip: Addition = add something across the double bond, which becomes a single bond. One in, one out. No atoms left over — everything ends up in the product.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students confuse addition and substitution reactions. In addition, NOTHING is removed — two things join to make one. In substitution (typical of alkanes with halogens), one atom replaces another.

keyPoints.heading: Addition polymerisation — making plastics from alkenes
keyPoints.content:
Addition polymerisation is a chain of addition reactions where many alkene monomer molecules join together to form a long polymer chain.

• The C=C double bond in each monomer opens up
• Each monomer links to the next, forming a long chain of −C−C− single bonds
• No atoms are lost — all monomer atoms end up in the polymer

Key examples:
• Ethene (C₂H₄) → poly(ethene) [polythene] — used for plastic bags, bottles
• Propene (C₃H₆) → poly(propene) [polypropylene] — used for ropes, food containers
• Chloroethene (vinyl chloride) → poly(chloroethene) [PVC]
• Tetrafluoroethene → poly(tetrafluoroethene) [PTFE / Teflon]

In the exam: You must be able to draw the repeat unit of a polymer from its monomer, and identify the monomer from the repeat unit.
writeBullets: Addition polymerisation: many alkene monomers join together (C=C opens up)
No atoms lost — all monomer atoms appear in the polymer chain
Ethene → poly(ethene); propene → poly(propene)
Polymer name: poly(monomer name)
Repeat unit drawn with bonds extending either side: −[−CH₂−CH₂−]n−
Monomer → repeat unit: replace C=C with −C−C−; add bonds either side

keyTerms.def: Small molecule with a C=C double bond that can join together to form a polymer
A long-chain molecule formed when many monomers join together; no atoms are lost in addition polymerisation
The smallest section of a polymer chain that, when repeated, gives the whole polymer structure
Polymerisation where alkene monomers add together via opening of C=C double bonds; no by-products

keyPoints.memTip: Polymer = poly + mer = many + parts. The monomer is the part, the polymer is the chain. The double bond opens to let each part link to the next.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Drawing repeat units is a regular exam question. Students often forget to draw the bonds extending from both ends of the bracket. Make sure the polymer chain shows bonds on both sides.

0.question: What is the general formula for alkenes?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Alkenes have the general formula CnH2n. For example: ethene C₂H₄ (n=2, 2×2=4 ✓), propene C₃H₆ (n=3, 2×3=6 ✓).
0.wrongFeedback: Alkenes: CnH2n. Alkanes are CnH2n+2. The difference is the C=C double bond — it 'uses up' two of the hydrogen positions, giving two fewer H atoms.

1.question: A student adds bromine water to an unknown compound. The orange bromine water turns colourless. What does this show?
options: The compound is unsaturated — it contains a C=C double bond (alkene)
The compound is saturated — it is an alkane
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Decolourisation of bromine water shows the compound is unsaturated — it has a C=C double bond. The bromine adds across the double bond.
1.wrongFeedback: Decolourisation of orange bromine water shows unsaturation — the compound has a C=C double bond (it is an alkene or other unsaturated compound). Alkanes do not react with bromine water at room temperature.

3.question: Ethene undergoes addition polymerisation. What type of molecules make up the polymer chain?
options: Repeat units derived from ethene monomers, joined by single C−C bonds
Ethene molecules still containing C=C double bonds
CO₂ and H₂O produced when ethene reacts with oxygen
Ethane molecules joined by hydrogen bonds
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right! Each ethene monomer's C=C double bond opens up, and the monomers link together into a long chain of −CH₂−CH₂− repeat units connected by single C−C bonds.
3.wrongFeedback: In addition polymerisation, the C=C double bond in each ethene monomer opens up and the monomers join together. The polymer (poly(ethene)) is a long chain of −CH₂−CH₂− units connected by single C−C bonds. No double bonds remain.

commonMistakes: Confusing the general formula — alkenes are CnH2n, not CnH2n+2 (which is alkanes). Ethene is C₂H₄, not C₂H₆.
Forgetting to describe the bromine water colour change fully — must say 'orange/brown to colourless', not just 'it changes colour'.
Saying alkanes decolourise bromine water — they do not at room temperature. Only alkenes (unsaturated compounds) do.
Drawing the polymer repeat unit without extending bonds either side of the brackets — the chain must show connections on both sides.
Confusing addition polymerisation with condensation polymerisation — addition polymerisation produces no by-products; all atoms from the monomers end up in the polymer.
examTips: For the bromine water test, always state the full colour change (orange to colourless) and what it shows (C=C double bond present / compound is unsaturated).
When drawing a repeat unit from a monomer, change the C=C to C−C and draw lines extending from both C atoms to show the continuing chain.
Addition reaction questions: the key phrase is 'the double bond opens up' — use this in your answer to show you understand the mechanism.
If asked to name the polymer from a monomer, put poly( ) around the monomer name: propene → poly(propene).
revisionCardBullets: Alkenes: unsaturated, contain C=C double bond
Bromine water test: orange → colourless = alkene (unsaturated)
Alkanes: bromine water stays orange (no reaction)
Addition reactions: C=C opens up, atoms add across it
Addition polymerisation: C=C opens, monomers link into polymer
